concerns.text: The plan proposes a 'post-hoc power analysis' to address sample size limitations. This is methodologically unsound for validating the study's primary claims. Post-hoc power is a function of the observed effect size and p-value; it cannot retroactively validate a study that was underpowered to begin with. The plan must either justify the sample size via an a priori power analysis (requiring an estimated effect size) or explicitly frame the study as exploratory with limited inferential power, rather than relying on post-hoc analysis to 'fix' the design.

The plan assumes 'mocked' inference or a 'quantized CPU model' for accuracy measurement. If the LLM inference is mocked (random or rule-based), the 'accuracy' metric loses construct validity as a measure of 'reasoning stability' or 'memory reconstruction quality.' If a quantized model is used, the CPU-only constraint (2 CPU, 7GB RAM) on GitHub Actions may introduce extreme latency variance or OOM errors, making the 'nodes_visited' vs. 'accuracy' trade-off confounded by system instability rather than algorithmic efficiency. The plan must define how 'accuracy' is measured in a way that is robust to the specific inference engine's limitations.

The plan proposes 'segmented regression' to identify a 'critical traversal depth threshold' (H3). This requires a sufficient density of data points across the range of 'nodes_visited' to detect an inflection point. If the 'Full' strategy always visits N nodes and 'Lazy' visits M nodes (where M is a fixed fraction of N), the data will be bimodal (two clusters) rather than continuous, rendering segmented regression invalid. The plan must ensure the heuristics produce a continuous distribution of traversal depths or use a different method (e.g., logistic regression with depth as a predictor) to test for thresholds.
